metadata.title: Why Steel-Manning the Opposition Makes Your Argument Harder to Attack
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut for the writing-guide book. Source chapter: writing-guide/chapters/12-position-argument-practicing-the-art-of-rhetoric.md (the objection section / steel-man vs straw-man mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = steel man (strong, honest engagement — earns skeptical reader trust); CRIMSON = straw man (softened, dismissible — loses the skeptical reader). GOLD = one editor-pen highlight, fill only, never text.
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: NO full position-argument structure overview, NO LBJ Great Society example, NO claim-construction mechanics, NO thesis-first vs delayed-thesis structure, NO voice/measured-advocacy section. One mechanism only: why the skeptical reader disengages when they see a weakened version of their own objection, and why steel-manning earns their trust at precisely that moment.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: You finish the draft. You've addressed the counterargument. But the skeptical reader has already stopped trusting you — and you don't know why.

card.copy: Why does addressing the counterargument sometimes make things worse?

beats.narration_text: Here is a student arguing against AI detection software. She writes an objection section. It looks thorough. It has two paragraphs.
beats.scene_description: A student at a desk, handwritten draft pages visible, late evening light — editorial newsprint treatment, desaturated warm tones
beats.image_prompt: B02, a college student at a wooden desk, 20s woman, draft pages of a written essay visible under her hands, warm desk lamp, late evening, slightly worried expression, editorial documentary photography, desaturated 80%, warm newsprint tones, flat editorial collage treatment
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] student at desk with draft — the setup

beats.narration_text: She writes: some might say cheating is bad, but detectors create problems too. Addressed. Refuted. Why does that lose the skeptical reader?

production_viz.label: the question card — straw man objection text + reader attention drop
production_viz.mechanic: serif quote card slides in: the straw-man objection text in full, then beneath it a reader-attention line drops to zero as the text is shown. A LabelChip 'THE QUESTION' appears top-left.
production_viz.note: THE QUESTION beat — must name the question on screen AND in narration

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] straw-man text card with reader-attention line dropping to zero

beats.narration_text: Every writing teacher says: address the counterargument. But the instruction says nothing about which version of it you have to address.

production_viz.label: two versions of the objection — straw (crimson, short) vs steel (teal, full)
production_viz.mechanic: two column panels appear side by side: left (CRIMSON chip: STRAW MAN) short bullet text; right (TEAL chip: STEEL MAN) longer, denser text. Both are labeled as 'the same objection'.
production_viz.note: establish the two-version distinction as the setup

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] split-panel: straw man vs steel man — the two versions

beats.narration_text: The straw man is built to knock down. Short. Soft. A version no serious critic would actually argue. Easy to dismiss — and visible from a mile away to anyone who holds the real objection.

production_viz.label: straw man anatomy — crimson, soft, dismissible
production_viz.mechanic: left panel zooms into focus: CRIMSON chip 'STRAW MAN', short objection text 'some might say cheating is bad, but detectors create problems too', a refutation text below that dismisses it in one sentence. A SerifLabel 'built to knock down' appears with crimson underline. A reader-attention line beneath starts neutral and drops as the refutation is delivered.
production_viz.note: the straw man anatomy: show what makes it weak

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] straw-man anatomy with reader-attention line declining

beats.narration_text: Here is what the skeptical reader actually believes: even imperfect detectors raise the cost of casual cheating enough that they reduce overall academic dishonesty — and the harms to non-native speakers are a known cost the institution has decided to accept.

production_viz.label: the steel man — the real objection, full-strength
production_viz.mechanic: right panel now zooms into focus: TEAL chip 'STEEL MAN', the full-strength objection text animates in line by line — the cost-of-cheating accounting, the institutions-decided-to-accept framing. A SerifLabel 'what serious critics actually argue' with teal underline. The text takes up more space; it looks serious.
production_viz.note: the steel man anatomy: show what makes it credible — it is specific, it accounts for costs

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] steel-man anatomy — the real objection text, full-strength

beats.narration_text: The mechanism: the skeptical reader is reading to see whether you have engaged with what they would say. The straw man tells them you haven't. They don't just disagree with your answer — they stop trusting the whole argument.

production_viz.label: reader-attention comparison — split screen with animated attention lines
production_viz.mechanic: FULL split screen. Left panel (CRIMSON label 'STRAW MAN'): straw-man text, reader-attention line starts mid-height then drops sharply to zero when the refutation dismisses it. Right panel (TEAL label 'STEEL MAN'): steel-man text, reader-attention line holds steady and rises slightly at the refutation. Both lines animate simultaneously. The contrast is the visual argument.
production_viz.note: this is the KEY ANIMATION — the visual argument for the whole film. Reader-attention as a moving line beneath each version.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] split screen — both reader-attention lines animate simultaneously

beats.narration_text: The straw man earns credibility only with the reader who already agrees with you. That reader was never the point. The position argument is designed to move the reader who doesn't.

production_viz.label: two readers: the already-convinced vs the skeptic
production_viz.mechanic: two StateCard-style entity cards side by side: left (INK/SLATE, label 'READER: AGREES') shows a check mark and the note 'straw man: still convinced'; right (CRIMSON label 'READER: SKEPTIC') shows the straw-man version and the note 'straw man: disengages here'. The right card's label turns crimson to show the cost.
production_viz.note: the implication — straw man works on one reader but abandons the one who matters

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two reader cards — who the straw man loses

beats.narration_text: Imagine Priya is arguing against AI detection software. Her steel-manned objection: even imperfect detectors raise the cost of casual cheating enough to reduce overall dishonesty. Her refutation: the false-positive rate at her university flagged 18 percent of multilingual student submissions — a specific harm the detector's benefit cannot absorb.

production_viz.label: the example: Priya's steel-manned objection + evidence-anchored refutation
production_viz.mechanic: a quote-card layout: top half shows the steel-man objection text (TEAL chip 'STEEL MAN'). Bottom half shows the refutation (a specific claim about false-positive rate). A GOLD highlight bar sweeps the specific evidence claim. SerifLabel 'illustrative example' at bottom in small text.
production_viz.note: Priya and the 18% figure are illustrative (labeled as such in FACTCHECK)

beats.narration_text: The move: before you write the objection section, write the strongest version of the opposing argument first — as if you hold it. Then ask: would the reader who holds this position feel it was taken seriously? If not, revise until they would.

production_viz.label: the practice move — two-step checklist
production_viz.mechanic: a two-step practice card. Step 1 (TEAL chip '1'): 'Write the strongest version of the opposing argument as if you hold it.' Step 2 (TEAL chip '2'): 'Ask: would the skeptical reader feel their view was taken seriously?' Both steps animate in with Create. Below: a SerifLabel 'If not — revise.' in TEAL with underline.
production_viz.note: THE PRACTICE beat — concrete, checkable, actionable. Not 'be careful'.

beats.narration_text: Steel-manning makes your argument harder to attack — not because it gives ground, but because it signals you did the work. The skeptical reader is looking for that signal.
beats.scene_description: A debate podium or writing desk scene — one person standing, confident, papers in hand, editorial newsprint treatment. The image conveys conviction earned through preparation.
beats.image_prompt: B11, a confident young person standing at a debate podium, papers in hand, determined expression, editorial documentary photography, desaturated 80%, warm newsprint tones, flat editorial collage treatment, overhead dramatic lighting

beats.narration_text: The question: why does engaging the strongest version of the opposing argument make your position harder to attack? The answer: because the skeptical reader is the only one whose trust earns you anything — and the straw man loses them before you reach your evidence.

card.copy: Steel-man earns the reader who disagrees.
card.sub: Straw-man earns only the one who already agrees.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — question restated, answer in one line
